front.title: Observations on the Reachability and Evasion of Packets with IPv6 Extension Headers on the Internet

abstract.t:
IPv6 Extension Headers (EHs) are designed to provide protocol flexibility and support for emerging features, while maintaining a concise base header and efficient processing. In practice, their reachability is affected by middlebox handling along the path, and their flexibility also introduces security considerations.
This document presents observations from a comprehensive, large-scale measurement study of IPv6 Extension Header path traversal across more than 23,000 autonomous systems. Using a feedback-driven measurement framework called 6Travel, the reachability of 11 common IPv6 Extension Headers is measured over ICMPv6, TCP, and UDP. The measurements indicate a change relative to earlier work: contrary to past observations of heavy filtering, specific Extension Headers now achieve reachability comparable to plain traffic. Two distinct forms of policy ossification are observed across industry categories, together with widespread potential Extension-Header-based firewall evasion signatures in nearly 5,000 autonomous systems, particularly under TCP and UDP. These signatures appear consistent with a combination of implementation flaws and security misconfigurations, spanning both on-path and host-side firewalls.
